stories.headline: Denver sets all-time March heat record at 87 degrees, fifth record-breaking day this month
stories.summary:
Denver International Airport recorded a high of 87 degrees Wednesday, shattering the previous record of 75 degrees set in 2012 and establishing a new all-time high temperature for the month of March. It marked the fifth record-breaking day in Denver this month alone.

A Red Flag Warning remains in effect for much of south-central Colorado on Thursday due to gusty winds, high temperatures, and critically low humidity. A cold front is expected to push through the state Thursday, bringing the chance of light showers mainly to the mountains and dropping Denver's high into the upper 70s to low 80s.

Temperatures are forecast to fall sharply by Friday, with highs only in the mid-to-upper 50s — closer to seasonal norms for late March. However, forecasters warn that warmer air and potentially more record-breaking heat are expected to return by the weekend.

stories.headline: Gov. Polis signs HOME Act and SB-1, expanding housing construction options for nonprofits and counties
stories.summary:
Gov. Jared Polis signed two housing bills into law Wednesday, House Bill 1001 — the HOME Act — and Senate Bill 1, describing them as the latest state efforts to address Colorado's high housing costs. The bills were the first measures introduced in the House and Senate respectively this legislative session.

The HOME Act allows nonprofits and certain government agencies, including RTD and school districts, to build housing on their own property. SB-1 gives counties the ability to spend property tax revenue on local housing authorities and workforce housing and to sell property for affordable housing development.

The legislation drew pushback from the Colorado Municipal League, whose executive director Kevin Bommer argued that HB-1001 imposes state mandates that bypass local planning authority. Polis, in his final year as governor, countered that the measures are necessary to break down barriers to affordable housing construction across the state.

stories.headline: Colorado 'No Kings' protests expected to draw tens of thousands Saturday, including major Capitol rally
stories.summary:
More than 70 "No Kings" protests are planned across Colorado on Saturday, March 28, as part of a nationwide wave of demonstrations opposing President Donald Trump's policies. Organizers estimate nearly 4,000 events are scheduled nationally.

The largest Colorado gathering is expected outside the State Capitol in Denver, where the Denver No Kings Coalition — comprising roughly three dozen local activist organizations — estimates attendance could surpass 70,000. Programming begins at 11:30 a.m. with singing and chanting, followed by speakers at noon and a 90-minute march through downtown at 1 p.m.

Events are planned statewide from Fort Collins and Steamboat Springs in the north to Trinidad in the south, as well as in western and eastern Colorado communities including Aspen, Montrose, La Junta, and Fort Morgan. Organizers describe the protests as "a collective stand against concentrated power, political intimidation, and systems that elevate the wealthy few over the many."

stories.headline: CBI issues Missing Indigenous Person Alert for 15-year-old Lakewood girl last seen in February
stories.summary:
The Colorado Bureau of Investigation issued a Missing Indigenous Person Alert Wednesday for Julianna Uong, 15, of Lakewood, who was last seen on Feb. 15 in the 2500 block of South Sheridan Boulevard. Authorities said she may currently be in the area of West Dartmouth Place and South Federal Boulevard in Denver and that law enforcement is concerned for her safety.

Julianna is described as 5 feet 5 inches tall, weighing 100 pounds, with black hair and brown eyes. She is Oglala Sioux and was last seen wearing black and red pajama pants and a black top. She may be wearing a gold necklace with her name and has a cherry tattoo on her left hand.

Anyone with information about Julianna's whereabouts is asked to call 911 or the Lakewood Police Department at 303-980-7300.

stories.headline: New 600-acre wildfire burns near Walsenburg in southern Colorado with no containment
stories.summary:
A wildfire broke out Wednesday afternoon in Huerfano County in southern Colorado, growing to more than 600 acres with no containment by Wednesday evening. The Turkey Fire was first reported at 2:22 p.m. near Huerfano County Road 122 and Ridge Road, approximately 10 miles northeast of Walsenburg and 4 miles south of the Pueblo County line.

Fire crews from the Gardner, Rye, and Spanish Peaks fire departments, along with a hotshot crew, were working the blaze as of Wednesday night. No containment had been established as of 8 p.m.

The fire comes amid an extended stretch of record-breaking heat, high winds, and critically low humidity across Colorado, conditions that fire officials have warned dramatically increase wildfire risk across much of the state.

stories.headline: Denver-based Ibotta signs exclusive multi-year digital promotions deal with Uber Eats and Postmates
stories.summary:
Denver-based digital promotions company Ibotta Inc. has signed an exclusive multi-year agreement with Uber Technologies to integrate its digital promotions platform into the Uber Eats and Postmates apps. The deal represents a significant expansion for Ibotta, which went public in 2024.

Financial terms of the agreement were not disclosed. The partnership would give Ibotta access to Uber's large base of food delivery customers across the United States.

The announcement adds to a busy period for Denver's technology sector and continues Ibotta's push to expand its network of retail and delivery platform partnerships beyond its core grocery and retail base.

stories.headline: Antitrust lawsuit filed against Vail Resorts and Alterra Mountain Co. over ski pass pricing practices
stories.summary:
A new antitrust lawsuit has been filed against Colorado-based Vail Resorts and Alterra Mountain Company, alleging the two ski resort giants colluded to inflate single-day lift ticket prices in order to push skiers into purchasing expensive season passes. The suit targets the Epic Pass, offered by Vail, and the Ikon Pass, offered by Alterra.

The lawsuit claims the pricing strategy harmed consumers by making one-day skiing prohibitively expensive, effectively forcing recreational skiers to buy costly multi-resort passes. Vail Resorts is headquartered in Broomfield; Alterra Mountain is based in Denver.

The companies together control a dominant share of major ski resorts across North America, including several of Colorado's most popular mountains. Neither company had issued a public statement on the lawsuit as of Wednesday.

stories.headline: Archbishop James Golka formally installed as leader of Denver's Archdiocese in ceremony at CoBank Arena
stories.summary:
James Golka was formally installed as the sixth archbishop of the Archdiocese of Denver on Wednesday in a ceremony at CoBank Arena at the National Western Center, attended by more than 4,000 people. Cardinal Christophe Pierre, the Vatican's U.S. ambassador, read the official letter from Pope Leo XIV confirming the appointment.

Golka, the former bishop of Colorado Springs, was appointed by the Vatican last February and now holds authority over approximately 600,000 Catholics in Northern Colorado, as well as serving as metropolitan archbishop for the dioceses of Colorado Springs, Pueblo, and Cheyenne, Wyoming. He succeeds Archbishop Emeritus Samuel J. Aquila, who retired after turning 75.

The installation was deliberately scheduled to coincide with the Feast of the Annunciation, a major Catholic solemnity. The ceremony was held at CoBank Arena rather than the Cathedral Basilica of the Immaculate Conception, which seats only 800 people.

stories.headline: National escape room company to open largest-ever location in RiNo at former 10 Barrel Brewing site
stories.summary:
The Escape Game, a national entertainment company with nearly 60 locations, has announced plans to open its largest location to date in Denver's RiNo neighborhood at 2620 Walnut St., the former home of 10 Barrel Brewing, which closed in late 2022. The 12,300-square-foot space will feature six escape game rooms and three game show rooms with live hosts.

Construction costs are estimated at approximately $1 million, and the location is expected to open by mid-2027. It will be Colorado's second Escape Game location, joining an existing site at Park Meadows Mall in Lone Tree.

The building is owned by Washington, D.C.-based real estate investment firm Edens, which has acquired more than $100 million in RiNo real estate since 2018 and has plans to redevelop a nearby block into new retail space including a grocery store. The Escape Game will not sell alcohol on-site but will offer a rooftop deck and lounge area with yard games and board games.

stories.headline: Broncos scouting running backs and other offensive positions ahead of NFL Draft, sources say
stories.summary:
The Denver Broncos are hosting top-30 draft visits with running back prospects Washington's Jonah Coleman and Indiana's Kaelon Black as they seek depth behind oft-injured starter J.K. Dobbins and second-year back RJ Harvey, according to sources who spoke with The Denver Post. The team also checked on free agent running back Chris Rodriguez Jr. before he signed with Jacksonville.

Coleman, a 5-foot-8, 220-pound power back, logged 15 rushing touchdowns and 31 receptions in 2025 and is considered a strong pass-protection prospect. He would likely be a target in the second round with Denver's No. 62 pick or in the fourth round. The Broncos also hold picks Nos. 108 and 111.

Beyond the backfield, Denver is conducting evaluations at tight end and offensive line heading into the April draft. Running back Dobbins, fresh off a contract extension, appeared on NFL Network this week touting the team's offensive weapons, which now include newly acquired wide receiver Jaylen Waddle.

stories.headline: Avalanche forward Logan O'Connor makes season debut after missing 69 games with mysterious injury
stories.summary:
Colorado Avalanche forward Logan O'Connor made his season debut Tuesday night in a 6-2 win over the Pittsburgh Penguins, returning after missing 69 games due to an injury that the team never fully disclosed. O'Connor, who had not played since Game 7 of the 2025 playoffs, logged more than 15 minutes of ice time after coach Jared Bednar initially planned to limit him to 8-10 minutes.

O'Connor contributed immediately, setting up a goal with a key pass, killing penalties, and forechecking throughout the game. Avalanche captain Gabe Landeskog praised O'Connor's return, saying his value to the locker room is difficult to quantify.

The undrafted free agent from the University of Denver is under a six-year contract with Colorado. His return comes at a critical time as the Avalanche push toward the playoffs. O'Connor also became a first-time father during his recovery, with son Brooks born in mid-November.

stories.headline: Rockies unveil full Opening Day roster featuring new starters Sugano, Lorenzen, and Quintana ahead of Friday's Miami opener
stories.summary:
The Colorado Rockies announced their 26-man Opening Day roster Wednesday ahead of Friday's season opener against the Miami Marlins, featuring three new veteran starting pitchers: Tomoyuki Sugano, Michael Lorenzen, and Jose Quintana. The additions are aimed at stabilizing a rotation that posted an all-time worst 6.65 ERA in 2025.

The Rockies also added outfielder Jake McCarthy, second baseman Willi Castro, and first baseman TJ Rumfield, all expected to start on Opening Day. The new front office, led by president of baseball operations Paul DePodesta, assembled the group as part of a broader rebuild after three consecutive 100-loss seasons, including a franchise-worst 119-loss campaign in 2025.

Denver native Kyle Freeland, the longest-tenured Rockie, will lead the rotation and must pitch 170 innings this season to trigger a $17 million vesting option. Colorado is trying to avoid becoming the first MLB team since the 1961-64 Washington Senators to suffer four consecutive 100-loss seasons.

weather.notable: About 29 degrees above the average high for this time of year!

joke.joke: A two-year-old is kind of like having a blender, but you don't have a top for it.

topStory.headline: Iran Reviews U.S. 15-Point Peace Proposal While Rejecting Direct Negotiations, Offering Its Own Counterterms
topStory.summary:
Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi said Wednesday that Tehran is reviewing a 15-point U.S. peace proposal to end the ongoing war, but stressed that an exchange of messages through intermediaries does not constitute direct negotiations with Washington. The Associated Press confirmed Iran has received the American plan, which President Donald Trump described Tuesday as part of active negotiations — a characterization Iran has flatly disputed.

Iranian state media outlets offered competing signals about Tehran's posture. State broadcaster Press TV, citing a senior political-security official, reported that Iran would reject the U.S. ceasefire offer and has put forward a five-point counteroffer of its own. Iran's counteroffer reportedly includes demands for control over the Strait of Hormuz, the world's most critical oil shipping chokepoint, as well as war-related reparations. A source cited by the Fars News Agency said Iran does not accept a ceasefire, arguing it is not logical to engage in such a process with parties it accuses of violating agreements.

The Strait of Hormuz demand appears likely to face significant resistance from Washington. Trump himself raised the idea Monday of joint control over the strait — shared between the United States and Iran's supreme leader — though no formal agreement on that point has been reported. The waterway is a vital artery for global oil shipments, and any arrangement granting Iran unilateral control would carry major economic and geopolitical implications.

The conflict between the United States, Israel, and Iran began on Feb. 28 with coordinated U.S. and Israeli strikes against the Islamic Republic. Pakistan has separately offered to facilitate talks between the two sides, according to the topic context, adding another potential diplomatic channel to an already complex picture. The gap between the two countries' stated positions — particularly on ceasefire terms, the Hormuz question, and whether any talks are even occurring — underscores the significant obstacles to a negotiated resolution.
